sections.name: system_prompt
sections.text: You are an LLM infrastructure assistant. Be precise, preserve critical constraints, and explain tradeoffs clearly.

sections.name: developer_instruction
sections.text: Always respect the requested output format. Prefer concise answers, mention uncertainty, and do not invent metrics that are not present in the prompt.

sections.name: user_question
sections.text: Given retrieved documents, recent chat history, and tool output, summarize the likely inference bottlenecks and recommend the safest next step.

sections.name: chat_history_old
sections.text:
User: Last week we discussed GPU utilization, speculative decoding, batching, and cache hit rates in several earlier experiments. Assistant: We also compared latency across multiple request sizes and talked about prompt engineering patterns that sometimes inflate input length without adding much signal.
User: I pasted a long incident summary with timestamps, queue depth metrics, cold-start observations, and several repeated notes about uneven latency across different traffic windows. Assistant: I responded with a detailed breakdown of request classes, average token lengths, and notes about how stale context can remain in chat history even when it is no longer needed for the current question.
User: We also reviewed old internal documentation excerpts, archived rollout plans, and repeated action items about throughput, prompt templates, historical dashboards, GPU memory pressure, noisy alert logs, and multiple environment snapshots from staging and production. Assistant: Much of that information helped at the time, but most of it is older context and should usually be dropped before more recent messages.
User: Here are several older snippets again for reference: the prefill phase became slower during one experiment, the decode phase became slower during another, retrieval chunks were duplicated accidentally, some prompt templates contained repeated boilerplate, and old tool outputs were copied into the same request even when they were no longer relevant. Assistant: This is exactly the kind of history that can dominate the prompt budget if it is kept forever.
User: Another archive note repeated many environment details such as request IDs, model aliases, benchmark labels, retry counters, batch sizes, cache warnings, and stale summaries from previous troubleshooting threads. Assistant: Those details may still be useful for audit purposes, but they are often much lower priority than the current task and the latest user question.
Archived turn 01: We investigated a staging issue, wrote a long status update, copied three dashboards into plain text, and preserved every metric name even after the problem changed. Archived turn 02: We repeated the queue depth explanation, the retry explanation, the timeout explanation, and the cache miss explanation in slightly different words. Archived turn 03: We copied benchmark notes for batch size 1, batch size 2, batch size 4, batch size 8, batch size 16, batch size 32, and batch size 64 even though the current prompt only needs the latest observation. Archived turn 04: We included a lengthy note about request fan-out, stale routing, cold pods, and log noise. Archived turn 05: We pasted older conclusions, newer conclusions, and then contradicted them with another retrospective paragraph.
Archive list: previous dashboard summary, previous on-call summary, previous notebook summary, previous benchmark summary, previous red-team summary, previous rollout summary, previous prompt-template summary, previous retrieval summary, previous latency summary, previous cache summary, previous GPU summary, previous CPU summary, previous scheduler summary, previous queue summary, previous timeout summary, previous retry summary, previous serialization summary, previous JSON summary, previous code summary, previous multilingual summary, previous punctuation summary, previous tokenization summary, previous context-window summary, previous agent summary, previous tool-calling summary, previous postmortem summary, previous experiment summary, previous migration summary, previous deprecation summary, previous backlog summary.
Additional archive narrative: The older thread kept repeating that the request contained copied logs, copied traces, copied IDs, copied warnings, copied chunk text, copied schema text, copied policy text, copied examples, copied metrics, copied rollout notes, copied comments, copied stack traces, copied exception blocks, copied retry details, copied environment notes, copied release notes, copied meeting summaries, copied action items, copied benchmark records, copied conclusions, copied reminders, copied TODO lists, copied caveats, copied historical context, copied side discussions, copied comparisons, copied analysis fragments, copied observations, copied hypotheses, copied dead ends, copied mitigations, copied workarounds, copied follow-ups, copied open questions, copied references, copied excerpts, copied tables, copied labels, copied tickets, copied incident summaries, copied result snippets, and copied debugging breadcrumbs.

sections.name: chat_history_recent
sections.text:
User: The newest logs show rising latency after adding long retrieval chunks. Assistant: That usually means prompt size, tokenization density, or larger output reservations are increasing effective workload. User: Please use that context when answering.
Assistant: The most recent runs suggest the issue is probably not raw GPU memory exhaustion, because memory stayed stable while input size rose. User: Right, and we also saw that structured tool results and repeated retrieval paragraphs pushed prompt tokens higher than expected. Assistant: Then the answer should focus on prompt composition, section priority, and why older chat history should be dropped before current tool output or current instructions.

sections.name: rag_chunk_1
sections.text: Chunk 1: The inference pipeline shows stable GPU memory but increased prefill time whenever the prompt contains several code blocks and JSON payloads in the same request. Additional retrieved notes explain that prompts with repeated operational checklists, long schema descriptions, and multiple surrounding examples can grow quickly even when the actual user question is small. Another section of the retrieved document says that prompt builders often concatenate system rules, developer instructions, multi-turn chat history, retrieval chunks, and structured tool output without measuring their combined token count first. The retrieval notes continue by describing how duplicated reference passages, repeated status summaries, and verbose safety wrappers can consume significant portions of the input budget before decoding starts.

sections.name: rag_chunk_2
sections.text: Chunk 2: Tokenizer comparisons suggest the same multilingual and tool-heavy prompt can produce very different token counts depending on the model family, affecting context usage before generation even begins. The retrieved material also highlights that long RAG prompts may include multiple overlapping chunks that repeat the same concepts in slightly different language. It warns that multilingual examples, copied logs, stack traces, JSON objects, and punctuation-heavy snippets tend to tokenize densely. The document recommends assigning explicit priority levels so that the prompt packer can keep the sections that define behavior and user intent while dropping redundant or older supporting context when budgets get tight.

sections.name: rag_chunk_3
sections.text:
Chunk 3: Older archived notes mention historical experiments on unrelated embedding recall benchmarks, several deprecated retrieval heuristics, and documentation excerpts that may be less relevant to the current latency issue. The archive includes long benchmark narratives, repeated meeting summaries, decommissioned pipeline diagrams described in text, and several paragraphs about experiments that are no longer active. Another archived section repeats observations about retrieval scoring, chunk overlap tuning, citation formatting, and legacy ranking strategies that are interesting background material but not essential for answering the current user question. The document ends with several old migration notes, rollout reminders, and retrospective commentary that should usually be treated as low-priority context.
Archived appendix A: A legacy retrieval run compared chunk size 128, 256, 384, 512, 768, and 1024 while recording observations about overlap, deduplication, metadata expansion, and citation post-processing. Archived appendix B: Another run described historical index refresh intervals, stale chunk eviction, rank fusion variants, long-form citation formatting, and deprecated scoring paths. Archived appendix C: Several paragraphs reviewed unrelated embedding regressions, index build times, vector store snapshots, and ranking annotations that are not necessary for the current prompt. Archived appendix D: Old troubleshooting notes described copied benchmark rows, copied profiler comments, copied incident snippets, copied runbook steps, copied migration cautions, and copied reminders about systems that no longer exist.
Low-priority archive list: old retrieval summary, old chunk overlap note, old citation note, old ranking note, old reranker note, old benchmark note, old relevance note, old heuristic note, old migration note, old quality note, old latency note, old schema note, old audit note, old support note, old annotation note, old cleanup note, old archive note, old comparison note, old rollout note, old fallback note, old patch note, old notebook note, old experiment note, old historical note, old backlog note, old maintenance note, old storage note, old ranking note again, old stale-index note, old duplicate-chunk note.
Overflow appendix: legacy note 01 about archived benchmarks, legacy note 02 about deprecated dashboards, legacy note 03 about removed heuristics, legacy note 04 about stale summaries, legacy note 05 about duplicated citations, legacy note 06 about experimental rank fusion, legacy note 07 about retired vector indexes, legacy note 08 about obsolete parser settings, legacy note 09 about superseded prompt formats, legacy note 10 about old migration warnings, legacy note 11 about unrelated alert reviews, legacy note 12 about copied benchmark snippets, legacy note 13 about old rollout tickets, legacy note 14 about repeated environment snapshots, legacy note 15 about historical ranking labels, legacy note 16 about stale archive comments, legacy note 17 about old notebook exports, legacy note 18 about prior deduplication attempts, legacy note 19 about deprecated fallback paths, legacy note 20 about duplicated storage notes, legacy note 21 about unrelated support tasks, legacy note 22 about outdated maintenance guidance, legacy note 23 about extra citation examples, legacy note 24 about older scoring commentary, legacy note 25 about repeated archive reminders, legacy note 26 about retired chunk templates, legacy note 27 about old audit breadcrumbs, legacy note 28 about verbose benchmark commentary, legacy note 29 about copied appendix text, legacy note 30 about stale retrospective notes.
Oversized archive tail: extra archive detail 31 about older scoring snapshots, extra archive detail 32 about retired chunk labels, extra archive detail 33 about duplicated migration notes, extra archive detail 34 about verbose incident commentary, extra archive detail 35 about older rollback plans, extra archive detail 36 about copied queue analyses, extra archive detail 37 about old evaluator settings, extra archive detail 38 about no-longer-used dashboards, extra archive detail 39 about repeated archive commentary, extra archive detail 40 about deprecated pipeline warnings, extra archive detail 41 about historical parser quirks, extra archive detail 42 about copied appendix bullets, extra archive detail 43 about archived ticket metadata, extra archive detail 44 about stale tuning experiments, extra archive detail 45 about retired benchmarks, extra archive detail 46 about older chunk templates, extra archive detail 47 about copied release notes, extra archive detail 48 about duplicated narrative context, extra archive detail 49 about historical trace exports, extra archive detail 50 about old storage summaries, extra archive detail 51 about older retry reports, extra archive detail 52 about repeated migration caveats, extra archive detail 53 about obsolete tagging notes, extra archive detail 54 about archived meeting commentary, extra archive detail 55 about stale reference blocks, extra archive detail 56 about old quality summaries, extra archive detail 57 about outdated fallback docs, extra archive detail 58 about duplicated archive warnings, extra archive detail 59 about old benchmark labels, extra archive detail 60 about no-longer-relevant background.

sections.name: final_instruction
sections.text: Return a short answer with three parts: bottleneck, evidence, and next step. If any section had to be dropped, say that the response may be missing older context.
